title: asyncio coroutines and tasks

canonicalExplanation.summary: An asyncio coroutine yields control at await points, tasks schedule coroutine progress on an event loop, and structured coordination must handle result, timeout, failure, and cancellation paths.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Three bounded simulated I/O calls run as tasks under a timeout, results are gathered, and remaining tasks are cancelled and awaited on failure. Your goal is to implement and trace a small asynchronous workflow with timeout and cancellation handling. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Typing, tests, logging and debugging; Generators and lazy pipelines; Concurrency versus parallelism; Exception handling and propagation. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: An asyncio coroutine yields control at await points, tasks schedule coroutine progress on an event loop, and structured coordination must handle result, timeout, failure, and cancellation paths. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Blocking calls freeze the event loop, creating a coroutine does not schedule it, cancellation is injected at suspension points, background tasks need strong references and observation, and shared state still races logically. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Declaring async makes the function run in parallel immediately and every call inside it becomes nonblocking.

includes: Use async, await and task coordination for bounded I/O-style examples without blocking the event loop.
Implement and trace a small asynchronous workflow with timeout and cancellation handling.
excludes: Blocking calls freeze the event loop, creating a coroutine does not schedule it, cancellation is injected at suspension points, background tasks need strong references and observation, and shared state still races logically.

outcomes: Implement and trace a small asynchronous workflow with timeout and cancellation handling.

examples.title: asyncio coroutines and tasks: direct language case
examples.situation: Three bounded simulated I/O calls run as tasks under a timeout, results are gathered, and remaining tasks are cancelled and awaited on failure.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: Three bounded simulated I/O calls run as tasks under a timeout, results are gathered, and remaining tasks are cancelled and awaited on failure.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Calling async creates a coroutine; schedule or await it, keep blocking work off the event loop, and handle timeout plus cancellation cleanup explicitly.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Calling async creates a coroutine; schedule or await it, keep blocking work off the event loop, and handle timeout plus cancellation cleanup explicitly.

examples.title: asyncio coroutines and tasks: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A blocking library call is moved to an appropriate thread boundary rather than called directly inside an async function.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A blocking library call is moved to an appropriate thread boundary rather than called directly inside an async function.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Blocking calls freeze the event loop, creating a coroutine does not schedule it, cancellation is injected at suspension points, background tasks need strong references and observation, and shared state still races logically.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Declaring async makes the function run in parallel immediately and every call inside it becomes nonblocking.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and trace a small asynchronous workflow with timeout and cancellation handling.

misconceptions.correction: Calling async creates a coroutine; schedule or await it, keep blocking work off the event loop, and handle timeout plus cancellation cleanup explicitly.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Declaring async makes the function run in parallel immediately and every call inside it becomes nonblocking.

analogy.example: a coordination desk that overlaps waiting jobs, assigns CPU work to separate rooms, and protects complete shared transitions can introduce asyncio coroutines and tasks, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: concurrency models, asyncio, threads, processes, cancellation, and safety
limitations: the event loop, GIL, serialization, atomicity, scheduling, and cancellation points must be reasoned about technically
